lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — The immunotherapy revolution
For most of cancer's history as a treated disease, doctors thought of the immune system as essentially irrelevant.
The standard model was: cancer is your own cells, the immune system doesn't really see them, our job is to physically remove or chemically kill the tumor.
That view was wrong, and the proof that it was wrong has become, in the last fifteen years, one of the most spectacular shifts in all of medicine.
The first hint came from a stubborn observation: rarely, in some cancers, the immune system spontaneously did kill the tumor.
Spontaneous remissions of melanoma happened, very occasionally.

Some kidney cancers regressed dramatically when patients were given immune-stimulating drugs called interleukins, despite those treatments being highly toxic and only working in a small percentage of patients.
Something was happening immunologically; we just couldn't reliably make it happen.

The breakthrough came from two scientists who eventually shared a Nobel Prize for it in 2018: James Allison in the United States and Tasuku Honjo in Japan, working separately on different molecules but arriving at the same insight.
They asked, essentially: what if the immune system already knows about the cancer but is being held back?
What if the problem isn't that T cells can't recognize the tumor, but that the tumor is sending T cells a "stand down" signal?
Recall the analogy from a few segments back.
Cancer cells display PD-L1 on their surface, which binds to PD-1 on T cells and tells the T cell to lower its weapon.
There's also a related signal involving CTLA-4 on T cells that acts as a brake earlier in the activation process.
Together these are called immune checkpoints — they're the natural brakes on the immune system that prevent it from attacking your own normal tissues.
Cancer cells learned to wave these flags to abuse the natural brake system.
The idea, then, was simple in hindsight and revolutionary at the time: design antibodies that block the checkpoint signals.
Take away the white flag.
Don't activate the immune system directly; just remove the brake the cancer has been using.

The drugs that came out of this idea — ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4), pembrolizumab and nivolumab (anti-PD-1), atezolizumab and several others (anti-PD-L1) — are called checkpoint inhibitors, and they have changed the prognosis for several cancers from "you have months to live" to "you may be in long-term remission.
The most dramatic story is melanoma.
Metastatic melanoma in the 1990s and 2000s was a near-death sentence; median survival was about nine months and almost no one was alive at five years.
Today, with combination checkpoint inhibitors, more than half of patients with metastatic melanoma are still alive five years out, and many of them appear to be effectively cured.
Their immune systems, once liberated from the checkpoint braking the cancer had been exploiting, finished the job.
Checkpoint inhibitors don't work for every cancer.

They work best in cancers that have many mutations — remember TMB-high and MSI-high — because those cancers display lots of weird protein fragments that the immune system can recognize once the brake is released.
They work poorly in cancers with few mutations, which present a smaller target.
And checkpoint inhibitors have a characteristic kind of side effect.
When you take the brakes off the immune system, the immune system sometimes attacks normal tissues too.
Patients can develop autoimmune-like conditions: inflammation of the thyroid, the lungs, the colon, the liver, the heart.
We call these immune-related adverse events, irAEs.
Most are manageable; some are severe.
The price of unleashing immunity against the cancer is that immunity sometimes goes after the patient as well.
Now, a different approach.
Checkpoint inhibitors release the brakes on T cells the patient already has.
CAR-T cell therapy takes a different tack: it engineers entirely new T cells to target the cancer.
The analogy I like: imagine your immune system has a security team patrolling for cancer, but the security team's eyesight is bad — they can walk right past the cancer without recognizing it.
CAR-T therapy gives a subset of those T cells a new pair of glasses — a genetically engineered receptor — so that they can now see, and attack, a specific cancer.
The procedure is wild.
We take T cells out of the patient's blood.
In the lab, we use a virus — usually an engineered HIV-derived lentivirus, which is a remarkable thing if you think about it — to insert a synthetic gene into those T cells.

The synthetic gene encodes a chimeric antigen receptor — CAR — that combines the targeting part of an antibody (which can be designed to recognize any protein) with the signaling part of a T cell receptor (which fires the killing program).
The result is a T cell that recognizes whatever target you've designed the antibody portion to bind, and kills it the moment it does.
We then expand those engineered cells, infuse them back into the patient, and they multiply inside the patient and hunt down the cancer.
The first CAR-T cells, approved in 2017, target a B-cell surface protein called CD19.
They were used for children and adults with B-cell leukemias and lymphomas who had failed every other treatment.
Patients with weeks to live experienced complete remissions.
Many of those patients are still in remission years later.
It's hard to overstate how unexpected this was.
CAR-T has its own dramatic side effects.

When the engineered cells start killing huge numbers of cancer cells fast, the immune system fires a massive cytokine storm — cytokine release syndrome, CRS — that can drop blood pressure, spike fevers, and damage organs.
We manage it with a drug called tocilizumab that blocks the inflammatory signaling.
There's also a neurological syndrome called ICANS that can cause confusion and seizures.
The therapies need ICU-level supportive care.
And CAR-T has limits.
It's worked beautifully for blood cancers expressing a few specific surface markers — CD19 for B-cell cancers, BCMA for myeloma.

Translating it to solid tumors — breast, lung, pancreas — has been much harder, partly because solid tumors don't have such clean surface markers and partly because the hostile microenvironment we discussed in Segment 9 quickly exhausts the engineered cells.
People are working on it.
Together, checkpoint inhibitors and CAR-T have created what's now called the immunotherapy era of oncology.
The frame has flipped.

We no longer think of cancer therapy as just chemistry against cells; we think of it as a contest between the cancer and the immune system, with our drugs as referees, removing handicaps from the immune side.
